summary: Joel Spolsky writes the foreword to Mike Gunderloy's 'Coder to Developer,' using his own career arc to illustrate how software developers consistently overestimate their knowledge. He recounts believing he knew everything after learning assembly, then after college, then after working at Microsoft and other companies — each time discovering vast gaps. He argues that computer science programs neglect practical skills like teamwork, UI design, source control, and business knowledge, dismissing them as 'vocational.' The core message is that writing code and developing software are fundamentally different disciplines, and true maturity begins with recognizing how much you don't know.
key_points: At every stage of a developer's career, there's a tendency to believe you already know everything about software development
Computer science departments at prestigious institutions refuse to teach practical skills like source control, bug tracking, UI design, and teamwork because they consider them 'vocational'
There's a peculiar quality about software that makes people with no training — including liberal arts majors at dotcoms — believe they can manage software teams and design interfaces
The gap between writing code and developing software is enormous — comparable to the difference between a child scrawling words and a successful novelist
True software development maturity begins with the humbling realization that you don't know what it really takes to develop software
money_quotes: What drives me crazy is that most software developers don't realize just how little they know about software development.
There's something weird about software development, some mystical quality, that makes all kinds of people think they know how to do it.

Nobody thinks they know how to remove a burst appendix, or rebuild a car engine, unless they actually know how to do it, but for some reason there are all these people floating around who think they know everything there is to know about software development.
Being a software developer means you can take a concept, build a team, set up state of the art development processes, design a software product, the right software product, and produce it.

Do those sound like the same thing? They're not. That's roughly the equivalent of going from being a six year old who can crayon some simple words, backwards N's and all, to being a successful novelist.

One day, finally, perhaps when it's too late, you'll wake up and say, 'Hmm. Maybe I really don't know what it really takes to develop software.' And on that day only, and not one minute before, you will have earned the right to call yourself a software developer.
key_insight: The Dunning-Kruger effect runs rampant in software development — true competence begins only when you recognize the vast gap between writing code and actually shipping quality software products.
